title: NIS2 Art.23 — Reporting Timeline Compliance Roll-Up (24h / 72h / 1-Month)
industry: All sectors in NIS2 Annex I and II
exclusions: Does not classify whether the incident was significant (relies on the lookup). Does not draft or submit reports. Does not handle CSIRT extensions outside the exception register or the artefact's `csirt_extension_granted_until` field. Does not fix process gaps — it surfaces them.
evidence: Saved search `UC-22.2.33` summary-indexed to `index=audit_evidence sourcetype=evidence:nis2_art23_rollup` (7-year retention, RFC 3161 TSA-signed via the saved-search alert action). Quarterly board pack PDF archived to `evidence:board_pack`. Dashboard `compliance/nis2-art23-board.xml` rendered to PDF for audit-committee review.

compliance.assurance_rationale: The UC produces an aggregate executive view of all three Art.23 deadlines (24h / 72h / 1-month) per significant incident, including the percentage of incidents that met each deadline over the trailing 90 days. Drafting and submission decisions remain outside Splunk; this UC measures and reports.
compliance.controlObjective: Proves an end-to-end picture of Art.23 reporting health: per significant incident, which deadlines were hit, missed, or extended; and per quarter, the operator's deadline-compliance rate trend.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search `UC-22.2.33` summary-indexed into `index=audit_evidence sourcetype=evidence:nis2_art23_rollup`. Quarterly board-pack PDF generated from the dashboard `compliance/nis2-art23-board.xml` and archived to `evidence:board_pack`.

controlTest.positiveScenario: Fixture `sample-data/uc-22.2.33-fixture.json` injects three significant incidents: (a) all three artefacts on time; (b) 24h ON_TIME, 72h LATE; (c) all three MISSED. The search returns three rows with the corresponding statuses and the aggregate `all_three_on_time` flag set correctly per row.
controlTest.negativeScenario: Same fixture but every incident has all three artefacts within deadline. The aggregate `any_missed=0` and `all_three_on_time=1` across all rows. The board-pack on-time % is 100%.

dataSources: ES `notable` macro for high/critical urgency notables; `index=audit_evidence sourcetype=evidence:nis2_artifact` (stages: `early_warning`, `72h_notification`, `final_report`, `crossborder_assessment`); `nis2_significant_incident_thresholds.csv`; `nis2_exception_register.csv`. Reads outputs of UC-22.2.1, UC-22.2.7, UC-22.2.8 indirectly via the artefact index.
app: Splunk Enterprise Security (Splunkbase 263) ≥7.0; Splunk Add-on for Phantom (Splunkbase 3411) ≥3.0; Splunk Common Information Model Add-on (Splunkbase 1621) ≥5.3.
spl:
`notable` urgency IN ("high","critical") earliest=-90d
| lookup nis2_significant_incident_thresholds.csv rule_name OUTPUT nis2_tier nis2_significant
| where coalesce(nis2_significant, "unclassified") != "no"
| eval detection_time = _time
| join type=left rule_name event_id [
    search index=audit_evidence sourcetype=evidence:nis2_artifact stage=early_warning earliest=-90d
    | stats latest(_time) as ew_time by rule_name event_id
  ]
| join type=left rule_name event_id [
    search index=audit_evidence sourcetype=evidence:nis2_artifact stage=72h_notification earliest=-90d
    | stats latest(_time) as not_time by rule_name event_id
  ]
| join type=left rule_name event_id [
    search index=audit_evidence sourcetype=evidence:nis2_artifact stage=final_report earliest=-90d
    | stats latest(_time) as fin_time by rule_name event_id
  ]
| eval ew_status = case(
    isnotnull(ew_time) AND ((ew_time - detection_time) / 3600) <= 24, "ON_TIME",
    isnotnull(ew_time), "LATE",
    (now() - detection_time) > 24*3600, "MISSED",
    1==1, "PENDING")
| eval not_status = case(
    isnotnull(not_time) AND ((not_time - detection_time) / 3600) <= 72, "ON_TIME",
    isnotnull(not_time), "LATE",
    (now() - detection_time) > 72*3600, "MISSED",
    1==1, "PENDING")
| eval fin_status = case(
    isnotnull(fin_time) AND ((fin_time - detection_time) / 86400) <= 30, "ON_TIME",
    isnotnull(fin_time), "LATE",
    (now() - detection_time) > 30*86400, "MISSED",
    1==1, "PENDING")
| eval all_three_on_time = if(ew_status="ON_TIME" AND not_status="ON_TIME" AND fin_status="ON_TIME", 1, 0)
| eval any_missed = if(ew_status="MISSED" OR not_status="MISSED" OR fin_status="MISSED", 1, 0)
| eval evidence_id = sha256(rule_name . event_id . _time . "rollup")
| table _time, rule_name, urgency, nis2_tier, owner, ew_status, not_status, fin_status, all_three_on_time, any_missed, evidence_id
| sort - _time
description: Produces a per-incident rollup of Art.23 deadline status (24h early-warning / 72h notification / 1-month final report) for the trailing 90 days, plus aggregate compliance percentages used in board reporting. Each row classifies each deadline as ON_TIME, LATE, MISSED, or PENDING with a single-pane evidence_id.
value: The Head of IR and the audit committee need a single page that says 'we hit X% of our 24h deadlines, Y% of our 72h, Z% of our 1-month over the last quarter'. Without this rollup the three per-stage UCs answer the operational question but cannot answer the governance question. This UC closes that gap and produces the board-pack data.
implementation: (1) Schedule daily at 03:00 over a 90-day window. (2) Summary-index findings to `audit_evidence sourcetype=evidence:nis2_art23_rollup` so trend queries can use `tstats` on the summary. (3) Generate a PDF dashboard digest weekly (audit committee monthly). (4) No paging — this is reporting, not operational.
visualization: Single value: 90-day on-time % for each stage. Stacked bar: weekly stage-status counts. Heatmap: per (urgency, nis2_tier) on-time rate. Table: per-incident rollup. Quarterly trend: rolling 4-week on-time %.
cimSpl: | tstats summariesonly=true count from datamodel=Alerts.Alerts where Alerts.severity IN ("high","critical") AND _time >= relative_time(now(), "-90d") by Alerts.signature, Alerts._time
dataModelAcceleration: Enable `Alerts` acceleration with 120-day retention; the rollup runs against the artefact index directly, but the cimSpl pre-filter speeds the notable enumeration.

references.title: ENISA NIS2 reporting templates and guidance

knownFalsePositives: 1) **Recently-onboarded data source — incidents detected in older systems before NIS2 reporting was operational** show MISSED for all three stages. Distinguish: `(_indextime - _time) > 7*86400`. Suppress: filter the rollup to incidents where detection time is within the operator's NIS2 onboarding date in `nis2_entity_scope.csv`. 2) **Late artefact backfill** — IR team manually backfills an artefact for a historical incident; the deadline calculation against `_time` shows LATE even though the artefact was filed immediately at the time of awareness. Distinguish: artefact `submission_was_backfilled=true` field. Suppress: in the SPL, treat backfilled artefacts as ON_TIME if `submission_was_backfilled` is true and an explanation is in `nis2_exception_register.csv`. 3) **Bulk re-classification** — incidents originally classified as non-significant later re-classified as significant; the deadline clock runs from re-classification, not original detection. Distinguish: artefact `stage=reclassification` for the event_id. Suppress: derive `detection_time` from the reclassification artefact's `_time`, not the notable's. 4) **Cross-border duplicates** — same incident raised in two member states; filing in either counts. Distinguish: `nis2_crossborder_primary.csv` lists the primary event_id. Suppress: deduplicate by `parent_incident_id` before the rollup. 5) **CSIRT-granted extensions for cross-border incidents** — extended deadlines are not breaches. Distinguish: `csirt_extension_granted_until` on the early-warning artefact. Suppress: use the extended deadline in the case() comparison. 6) **`PENDING` for an incident still in active response** — these are not failures, they are in-flight. Distinguish: `(now() - detection_time) < 24*3600` for the 24h stage, etc. Suppress: the rollup correctly distinguishes PENDING (in-flight) from MISSED (deadline passed); board-pack reporting uses MISSED, not PENDING.
equipmentModels: Splunk Enterprise / Splunk Cloud (scheduled searches and audit_evidence index)
Splunk Enterprise Security 7.x (Splunkbase 263, incident response and evidence archive)
detailedImplementation:
## Prerequisites

• **Splunk**: Enterprise ≥9.2 or Splunk Cloud (current). Scheduling requires a search head not under memory pressure (check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=ProcessRunner` for OOM warnings before deploying a new scheduled search).
• **Indexes**: `audit_evidence` must exist on all indexers with retention matching the regulation's minimum (PCI: 1y, HIPAA: 6y, NIST 800-53: 1-3y per FIPS 199, SOX: 7y). Set `frozenTimePeriodInSecs` accordingly in `indexes.conf`.
• **Lookups**: `nis2_significant_incident_thresholds.csv` must be deployed to the search-tier app and refreshed on a documented schedule. For KV Store lookups, verify with `| rest /servicesNS/-/-/data/transforms/lookups | search name IN ('nis2_significant_incident_thresholds.csv')`.
• **Search-head capacity**: Subsearch-based detections need `subsearch_maxtime` ≥ 60s and `subsearch_maxout` ≥ 50000 in `limits.conf`. For high-volume environments, tune `[search] max_searches_per_cpu` up from default.
• **Regulatory mapping**: This detection evidences clause `Art.23`. The control objective is: "Proves an end-to-end picture of Art.23 reporting health: per significant incident, which deadlines were hit, missed, or extended; and per quarter, the operator's deadline-compliance rate trend." — every alert payload should include this text so downstream consumers know the compliance purpose.

## Step 1 — Configure data collection

Data collection is bespoke for this UC — no single canonical TA parses every source the detection relies on. Route data into `audit_evidence` under `evidence:nis2_artifact`, and document the ingestion path in the UC runbook so future engineers know where each field originates.


Four distinct operational considerations shape this detection's rollout:


• **Hint 1:** Schedule daily at 03:00 over a 90-day window. Schedule cadence is a tradeoff between regulatory latency and search-head load; document the chosen cadence and the reasoning in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 2:** Summary-index findings to `audit_evidence sourcetype=evidence:nis2_art23_rollup` so trend queries can use `tstats` on the summary. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 3:** Generate a PDF dashboard digest weekly (audit committee monthly). Report-generation cadence must match the regulatory reporting cycle; a monthly digest satisfies an annual review requirement but not a quarterly one.

• **Hint 4:** No paging — this is reporting, not operational. Report-generation cadence must match the regulatory reporting cycle; a monthly digest satisfies an annual review requirement but not a quarterly one.

## Step 2 — Implement the search

For "NIS2 Art.23 — Reporting Timeline Compliance Roll-Up (24h / 72h / 1-Month)," the aggregation is the evidence artifact itself — auditors will read these rows as the official record of NIS2 control activity.

This search correlates the outer dataset (index=`audit_evidence`, sourcetype=`evidence:nis2_artifact`) with a subsearch on `rule_name event_id` across the last 90 days. `type=left` preserves all rows from the outer search; nulls in the inner side indicate missing data that may itself constitute a finding.

Join caveat: Splunk's `| join` subsearch is capped at `max_searches_per_cpu * subsearch_maxout` results (default: 50000). If the inner subsearch exceeds this, rows are silently dropped and the resulting report will under-count. Check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd log_level=WARN subsearch` for truncation warnings. For inner searches expected to return >10000 rows, rewrite as `| stats` with a larger base search and post-filter, or use `| tstats` if a CIM datamodel covers the data.

**CIM / accelerated variant:** The detection also has an accelerated variant using `| tstats` against the CIM `Alerts` datamodel:

```spl
| tstats summariesonly=true count from datamodel=Alerts.Alerts where Alerts.severity IN ("high","critical") AND _time >= relative_time(now(), "-90d") by Alerts.signature, Alerts._time
```

When to prefer this variant: once the underlying datamodel is accelerated (verify in Settings > Data Models > `Alerts` > status=100%), this query runs 10-50x faster than the raw variant and can feasibly cover multi-month windows for audit reporting. When to avoid: if your field extractions are app-specific and the CIM tagging is incomplete, the `tstats` variant will silently under-count. Always compare raw vs accelerated counts on a known day before swapping the production schedule. Maintain both variants in the UC — the raw is the primary for accuracy, the accelerated is the performance optimisation for dashboarding.

**Schedule configuration** (`savedsearches.conf`):
```ini
[UC-22.2.33: NIS2 Art.23 — Reporting Timeline Compliance Roll-Up (24h / 7]
cron_schedule = 0 3 1 * *
dispatch.earliest_time = -90d
dispatch.latest_time = now
actions = summary_index
action.summary_index._name = summary_compliance
action.summary_index.marker = uc=22.2.33,reg=NIS2
```

## Step 3 — Validate deployment

**(a) Field extraction check:** Run the following to confirm the TA is extracting every field the detection relies on:

```spl
index=audit_evidence sourcetype=evidence:nis2_artifact earliest=-1h
| fieldsummary
| where count=0
| table field
```

Any row returned is a field the TA claims to extract but isn't appearing in recent data — it's either a rename upstream, a TA version mismatch, or a data-format change.

**(b) Required-field presence:** This detection specifically needs: `_time`, `rule_name`, `urgency`, `nis2_tier`, `owner`, `ew_status`, `not_status`, `fin_status`, `all_three_on_time`, `any_missed`. Run:

```spl
index=audit_evidence sourcetype=evidence:nis2_artifact earliest=-1h
| head 100
| table _time, rule_name, urgency, nis2_tier, owner, ew_status, not_status, fin_status
```

Every row should have non-null values for the fields the detection's `where` clause filters on. Nulls cause silent drops in the result set.

**(c) Lookup freshness:** The detection joins against `nis2_significant_incident_thresholds.csv`. Check its modification time and row count:

```spl
| inputlookup nis2_significant_incident_thresholds.csv
| eval _lookup_row=1
| stats count as rows
```

Compare the row count to the expected baseline; an unexpected drop is a sign the upstream export truncated.

**(e) Negative test:** Generate a synthetic 'should not alert' event using the Splunk Sample Generator or a crafted `| makeresults` event and confirm the detection does NOT fire on it. A detection that fires on everything is equivalent to a detection that fires on nothing.

## Step 4 — Operationalize (dashboard and reporting)

Create a companion dashboard titled `UC-22.2.33: NIS2 evidence` with the following panel layout:

**Panel 1 — Single Value: 90-Day On-Time % For Each Stage. Stacked Bar: Weekly Stage-Status Counts. Heatmap: Per (Urgency, Nis2 Tier) On-Time Rate. Table: Per-Incident Rollup. Quarterly Trend: Rolling 4-Week On-Time % (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.


Time picker defaults: set to last 30 days with named presets for 24h, 7d, 30d, 90d, and 1y — the auditor needs all five windows without retyping. Enable scheduled PDF export for the full dashboard on the regulation's reporting cadence.

## Step 5 — Troubleshooting and regulation-specific caveats

**No results when violations exist:** The most likely cause is subsearch truncation (see Step 2). Inspect `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=searchprocessor log_level=WARN subsearch` for messages like 'Output result count exceeded 50000.'

**Search times out:** Check the search job inspector (`Activity > Jobs > <this UC's last run> > Inspect`). The phase consuming the time (fetch, command, summary) indicates whether the issue is data-layer (too wide a window, unaccelerated datamodel), command-layer (expensive `transaction` or `eventstats`), or post-aggregation.


**NIS2-specific failure modes:**

• 24/7 reporting obligation (Art. 23): NIS2 requires early warning within 24 hours of awareness of a significant incident. Detections that back this SLA must compute elapsed time from the SOC's first-acknowledged timestamp (not the resolve-time) and must trigger independently of business hours — the regulatory clock does not pause overnight.

• Member-state variation: NIS2 is transposed individually by each EU Member State with some latitude on thresholds and sector scoping. A detection correct for German IT-SiG 3 (BSI) may be misaligned with Dutch CWV or French LPM. Use `nis2_member_state_config.csv` to parametrize thresholds per jurisdiction; never hardcode.

• Essential vs Important entity criteria: the applicable obligations depend on sector + size. Before alerting, verify the monitored entity's designation; a detection firing on an out-of-scope subsidiary causes unnecessary regulatory notifications that erode trust with the national CSIRT.

• Supply-chain (Art. 21(2)(d)) extent: NIS2 explicitly includes direct suppliers' cybersecurity posture. Detections must cover monitored-by-you-not-owned-by-you infrastructure (outsourced SaaS, managed SIEM tenants) — these are in scope even if operationally run by a third party. BA/BAA-style governance applies.
grandmaExplanation: Every quarter, the audit committee asks: 'Did we send the regulator our cyber incident updates on time?' This watch tracks every incident's three deadlines — 24 hours, 72 hours, one month — and produces the percentage of incidents that hit each deadline. It is the report card.
